findings.issue: Core agent loop (run_unified) has zero integration tests. This is the central execution path orchestrating LLM calls, tool dispatch, stuck detection, mid-stream messaging, and cost tracking — yet the only agent tests are for the reflection loop abstraction and the repetition detector sub-component. The critical 1000+ line run_unified function is entirely untested.
findings.recommendation: Add at minimum: (1) a mock-LLM integration test that verifies a single-turn run produces a Complete event, (2) a multi-turn test that exercises tool execution, (3) a max_turns limit test, (4) a mid-stream steering queue test. Use the existing MockProvider pattern from ava-llm tests.

findings.issue: Permission middleware (the approval bridge that intercepts tool calls requiring user approval) has no dedicated unit tests. The permission_system integration tests cover the inspector and classifier, but the middleware pipeline that actually gates tool execution in production is not exercised by any test.
findings.recommendation: Add tests that verify: (1) write/edit tools are intercepted and approved before execution, (2) auto-approve mode bypasses the bridge, (3) critical commands are blocked even in auto-approve mode, (4) denial propagates a ToolError rather than silently succeeding.
findings.code_snippet: // crates/ava-tools/src/permission_middleware.rs — no #[cfg(test)] module

findings.issue: AgentStack::run() (the public entry point used by both TUI and Tauri) has no tests at all. This integrates the model router, tool registry, session manager, memory system, MCP runtime, plugin manager, context condensation, and permission system — the entire backend pipeline — with zero test coverage.
findings.recommendation: Add a smoke-test using AgentStackConfig with a mock/ollama provider to verify the stack initialises, accepts a goal, completes a single turn, and returns a session with at least one message.

findings.issue: WasmLoader::load() is a public API that always returns Err(Unsupported) with a comment 'TODO: Integrate with wasmtime'. It is advertised in CLAUDE.md as supported (WASM extensions), but the implementation is a permanent stub. Callers have no way to distinguish 'not yet implemented' from a runtime error.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) gate the type behind a cargo feature flag `wasm-extensions` and return a compile error when the feature is absent, or (b) document explicitly in the public API that WASM is not yet supported and move the TODO to the backlog. Remove the public type from the crate's exported API until implemented.

findings.issue: Plugin request-header injection hook is documented as a TODO inline on the public LLMProvider trait definition. This means every LLM API request bypasses the plugin hook system entirely — plugins cannot add custom auth headers, tracing headers, or modify requests. The comment is on a public trait, making it a documented but silently broken contract.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) implement the RequestHeaders hook by passing an optional PluginManager reference through the provider creation call site, or (b) remove the TODO comment and file a tracked issue, since an in-code TODO on a public trait misleads contributors about what is actually wired.
findings.code_snippet:
// TODO: Wire ava_plugin::HookEvent::RequestHeaders hook here to allow plugins
// to inject custom headers into LLM API requests.

findings.issue: Shell injection risk via sh -c composition. The lint and test_runner tools build commands by string concatenation and execute them via sh -c, with a TODO acknowledging this should use direct argv builders instead. The validate_custom_command() guard is the only protection, which is a denylist approach.
findings.recommendation: Replace sh -c string execution with tokio::process::Command with explicit argv for known toolchain commands (cargo, npm, eslint, etc). Reserve sh -c only for truly custom user-provided commands with explicit allowlist validation.
findings.code_snippet:
// TODO: Execute test frameworks via direct argv builders instead of sh -c
// This prevents shell injection from test/lint command composition.

findings.issue: Identical TODO and identical detect_*_command implementation as lint.rs — the shared project-tooling detector is duplicated across both files with a TODO noting the DRY violation. This means any bug fix or new toolchain detection must be applied in two places.
findings.recommendation: Extract a shared detect_toolchain_command() function into crates/ava-tools/src/core/toolchain.rs that both LintTool and TestRunnerTool consume.
findings.code_snippet: // TODO: Factor out shared project-tooling detector (DRY with lint.rs)

findings.issue: Legacy module with a known correctness issue: list_by_session orders by created_at which can have timestamp ties, causing non-deterministic message ordering. The TODO has been present since the module was created and the module header explicitly labels it 'Legacy'. However, it is still a pub API with working insert/get methods that downstream code could call.
findings.recommendation: Add a created_seq INTEGER DEFAULT rowid column to the messages table migration and update list_by_session to ORDER BY created_seq ASC. Alternatively, deprecate the module with a #[deprecated] attribute pointing to ava-session as the canonical store.
findings.code_snippet:
// TODO: Add per-session sequence column for stable ordering.
// Currently orders by created_at which can have ties.

findings.issue: Two #[allow(dead_code)] suppressions on public-facing AgentStack fields: `yolo: bool` (retained for future use) and `shared_tool_sources: SharedToolSources` (stored for future dynamic-registration updates). Dead fields on the primary application struct inflate memory layout and confuse new contributors about the intended API surface.
findings.recommendation: Remove the `yolo` field entirely — the feature is now PermissionLevel::AutoApprove. Move `shared_tool_sources` to a private helper type or add a concrete use site within the same sprint cycle.
findings.code_snippet:
#[allow(dead_code)] // Field retained for future use; accessor removed as dead code
yolo: bool,
#[allow(dead_code)] // Stored for future dynamic-registration updates
shared_tool_sources: SharedToolSources,

findings.issue: HttpTransport is a public type whose send() silently discards all messages (let _ = message; Ok(())) and whose receive() always returns an error. This is documented as intentionally incomplete, but it is exported as a public type in a stable crate. Any caller building on this API will get silent data loss from send().
findings.recommendation: Gate HttpTransport behind a #[cfg(feature = "http-transport")] unstable feature flag, or replace the send() no-op with Err(AvaError::ToolError("HTTP send not implemented".into())) to fail loudly rather than silently.

findings.issue: CodeSearchTool rebuilds the entire CodebaseIndex on every invocation. The TODO acknowledges this is a performance issue. For large projects this creates a multi-second latency on every code_search call, making it effectively unusable in agentic loops.
findings.recommendation: Pass a shared Arc<RwLock<Option<Arc<CodebaseIndex>>>> into CodeSearchTool at construction time (same pattern used by AgentStack for codebase_index) and reuse the index built during stack initialization.

findings.issue: Two #[allow(dead_code)] suppressions on WebStateInner fields question_rx and approval_rx, with comments 'auto-answer for now in web mode'. These are critical interactive features (user questions and tool approval) that are silently auto-answered in the web UI, making the web mode unsafe for write/destructive tool operations.
findings.recommendation: Implement proper WebSocket-based question/approval request forwarding rather than silently dropping these receivers. Until implemented, document in the web server startup log that interactive approvals are auto-approved in web mode.
findings.code_snippet:
#[allow(dead_code)]
pub question_rx: tokio::sync::Mutex<mpsc::UnboundedReceiver<QuestionRequest>>,
#[allow(dead_code)]
pub approval_rx: tokio::sync::Mutex<mpsc::UnboundedReceiver<ApprovalRequest>>,

findings.issue: validate_targets() method on RoutingConfig is a no-op that only logs a debug message. The TODO says to wire it into config loading once the model registry is available. The method is called at startup but performs no validation, meaning invalid routing targets (typos in provider/model names) are silently ignored until a runtime routing failure occurs.
findings.recommendation: Wire validate_targets() to the model registry singleton (which is initialized before config validation runs) and emit tracing::warn! for unknown provider/model combinations. Add a unit test that exercises the validation path.

findings.issue: Sandbox model is inverted: only install-class commands are sandboxed, all other bash commands run unsandboxed. The TODO acknowledges this should be inverted — sandbox all commands by default with an allowlist. In the current model, arbitrary shell commands (rm, curl, git push) execute without sandbox constraints.
findings.recommendation: Implement a sandbox-by-default policy: run all bash commands through the sandbox, with a statically allowlisted set of development commands (cargo, npm, git status/log/diff, etc.) that bypass the sandbox. This aligns with the PermissionLevel::AutoApprove design where critical commands are still blocked.

findings.issue: RepetitionDetector::reset() is marked #[allow(dead_code)]. The method exists but is never called during agent loop execution — the detector is only created fresh per run_unified() call. This means the reset mechanism is dead infrastructure.
findings.recommendation: Either call reset() at the appropriate point in the agent loop (e.g., after a steering message is received, since the user has redirected the agent) or remove the method if it serves no purpose.

findings.issue: resolve_data_dir() in the web module is marked #[allow(dead_code)] and uses unwrap_or_default() on home_dir(). The function is never called, and if it were, a missing home directory would silently use the current directory as the data directory, potentially writing user data to unexpected locations.
findings.recommendation: Remove the unused function, or if it is intended for future use, replace unwrap_or_default() with proper error propagation and make it return Result<PathBuf>.

findings.issue: ClaudeCodeResponse and ClaudeCodeUsage have multiple #[allow(dead_code)] fields (session_id, cache_read_input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens). The cache token fields are important for cost tracking — Anthropic's prompt caching data is being deserialized but then silently discarded.
findings.recommendation: Wire cache_read_input_tokens and cache_creation_input_tokens into the cost calculation path used by the rest of the system (TokenUsage struct has cache_read_tokens and cache_creation_tokens fields). Remove the dead_code suppression once wired.
findings.code_snippet:
#[allow(dead_code)]
cache_read_input_tokens: Option<u64>,
#[allow(dead_code)]
cache_creation_input_tokens: Option<u64>,

findings.issue: AgentLoop has no doc comment on the struct itself despite being the core execution engine. New contributors reading the code have no high-level description of the lifecycle, state machine, or how run_unified() relates to run() and run_streaming().
findings.recommendation: Add a module-level doc comment to agent_loop/mod.rs and a struct-level doc comment to AgentLoop explaining the three-tier message queue, the unified engine pattern, and the relationship to AgentStack.

findings.issue: Middleware trait (in ava-tools) has no doc comment on the before() method explaining what it should do on failure — should it return Err to abort the tool call, or is that reserved for after()? The asymmetry between before() returning Result<()> and after() returning Result<ToolResult> is also undocumented.
findings.recommendation: Add doc comments to both Middleware methods clarifying: (1) Err from before() aborts tool execution and returns the error as ToolResult.is_error, (2) Err from after() replaces the tool result, (3) the priority field governs ordering when multiple middlewares are registered.

findings.issue: FTS5 index TODO for learned memories. The learned memory query path uses a linear scan (no FTS5) with an acknowledged TODO. As the learned memory store grows, this degrades to O(n) on every memory enrichment call that happens at the start of each agent run.
findings.recommendation: Add a CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE learned_fts USING fts5(content, tokenize='porter ascii') migration and update the query to use FTS5 matching. This is especially important since memory enrichment runs on the hot path before every agent turn.
findings.code_snippet: // TODO: Add FTS5 index for learned memories if this query path becomes hot.

findings.issue: Snapshot ref cleanup/retention is an unimplemented TODO tagged sprint-61. Without cleanup, git snapshot refs accumulate indefinitely in the repository, polluting the ref namespace and increasing repo size over time.
findings.recommendation: Implement a retention policy: keep the last N snapshots per session (configurable, default 10), and schedule cleanup either at AgentStack shutdown or via a background task. This was sprint-scoped so it is expected to be picked up soon.
findings.code_snippet: /// TODO(sprint-61): add snapshot ref cleanup/retention once recovery UX lands.

findings.issue: AgentLoop tests only cover sub-components (repetition detector, reflection loop) and session_logger. There is no test that verifies the dedup guard (last_request_hash) prevents duplicate requests, which was a known bug fix (the dedup guard was fixed to include msgs.len()). Regression risk is high.
findings.recommendation: Add a test using a counting MockProvider that verifies sending the same goal twice with the same context only makes one LLM call (dedup guard fires on second attempt), but sending after adding a new message makes a second call.
findings.code_snippet:
pub(crate) last_request_hash: Option<u64>,
pub(crate) last_request_time: Option<Instant>,
